title: Tabbouleh
subtitle: Lebanese parsley and bulgur salad
excerpt: Tabbouleh is a Lebanese herb salad: predominantly flat-leaf parsley, with a small amount of bulgur wheat, tomato, spring onion, and mint dressed in lemon and olive oil. The parsley is the point — this is not a grain salad with herbs in it but an herb salad with a little grain. The ratio matters.
sourceNotes:
• Claudia Roden, *A New Book of Middle Eastern Food* (1985). Principal reference for the parsley-forward ratio and the fine-chop technique.
• Anissa Helou, *Lebanese Cuisine* (1994). Cross-reference for the bulgur soaking method and the spring onion to tomato proportions.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Tabbouleh improves after 30 minutes as the bulgur absorbs the lemon juice. Make up to 2 hours ahead but add tomatoes just before serving to prevent them making the salad watery.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Lebanese herb salad made predominantly from finely chopped flat-leaf parsley, with bulgur wheat, tomato, spring onion, mint, lemon, and olive oil. The key distinction from Western adaptations is the ratio: parsley dominates; grain is a background element.

content.text: is frequently misunderstood. Supermarket versions and many Western adaptations treat it as a bulgur wheat salad with parsley added. The Lebanese original is the reverse: an enormous quantity of very finely chopped flat-leaf parsley, with a small amount of soaked bulgur mixed through. The recipe below reflects that ratio — three large bunches of parsley for 60 g of grain.

The chopping is the work. The parsley should be chopped until the leaves are quite fine — not a rough tear, but a proper knife cut that releases the oils without bruising to mush. A food processor makes the parsley watery and uneven; a sharp knife over a large board is the right tool. Allow 15 minutes for the chopping alone.

items.prepNote: leaves and fine stems only, very finely chopped

content.text: A large board and a sharp cook's knife are the essential tools. The bulgur needs no cooking — soaking in cold or warm water for 20 to 30 minutes is sufficient for fine-grade bulgur.

Put {{bulgur-wheat}} of fine bulgur in a small bowl and cover with cold water. Leave for 20 to 30 minutes until the grains have swollen and softened. Drain through a fine sieve, pressing out as much water as possible. Spread on a clean tea towel and pat dry.

Chop {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley leaves very finely using a sharp knife. This is the longest step and is done by hand — not in a processor. Chop {{mint}} of mint leaves finely. Slice {{spring-onion}} spring onions as finely as possible. Deseed and dice {{tomato}} tomatoes into small cubes; deseed to prevent the salad becoming watery.

Combine the parsley, mint, spring onion, and drained bulgur in a large bowl. Add the juice of {{lemon}} lemons and {{olive-oil}} of extra-virgin olive oil. Season generously with salt and pepper and toss well. Leave to rest for 30 minutes at room temperature — this allows the bulgur to absorb the dressing and the flavours to develop. Add the tomatoes just before serving and toss again.

items.cause: Tomatoes were added early and released their juice, or bulgur was not dried well enough after soaking.
items.fix: Always deseed the tomatoes and add them at the last minute. Pat the soaked bulgur as dry as possible before mixing — excess water dilutes the dressing and softens the herbs.

items.cause: Too many thick stems were included, or the parsley is old.
items.fix: Use only the leaves and the very fine upper stems. Thick stems are bitter. Fresh parsley is essential — old parsley loses its brightness and tastes grassy.

items.cause: Not enough lemon juice, or the lemon was added too early and lost its lift.
items.fix: Taste and add more lemon juice just before serving. A dish this simple relies entirely on the quality and quantity of its dressing.

content.text: Tabbouleh is the national dish of Lebanon and a staple across Syria, Jordan, and Palestine. It appears on the table at almost every Lebanese meal as part of the meze spread, alongside hummus, baba ganoush, and flatbread. In Britain it became well known through the expansion of Lebanese and Middle Eastern restaurants from the 1970s, and is now widely available in supermarkets — though almost always in the grain-forward version that misrepresents the original. Making it at home with the correct parsley ratio is the restoration.
